1:
"Then verily the first covenant had also ordinances of divine service, and a worldly sanctuary." The brief verse notes the first covenant's ordinances and earthly sanctuary. The grammatical voices note the old order. Barnes and Robertson note the writer turning to describe the old covenant's worship: "the first covenant had also ordinances (dikaiomata — regulations, prescribed rites) of divine service (latreias — worship)" — the old covenant came with its appointed rules and ceremonies of worship.

On "ordinances of divine service," the witnesses note the prescribed worship. Barnes and Calvin note that the old covenant's worship was carefully regulated by divine ordinance — every rite, sacrifice, and ceremony prescribed; an elaborate, God-given system of service.

On "a worldly sanctuary," the grammatical-historical note the earthly tabernacle: "a worldly (kosmikon — of this world, earthly, material) sanctuary" — the old covenant had a material, earthly sanctuary (the tabernacle), belonging to this world; the writer will describe it (vv.2-5) to draw out its typical meaning over against the heavenly. The verse displays the first covenant's ordinances and earthly sanctuary.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the old order — "the first covenant had also ordinances (prescribed rites) of divine service (worship)," its worship regulated by divine ordinance (Barnes, Robertson); an elaborate God-given system of rites and ceremonies (Barnes, Calvin); "a worldly (earthly, material) sanctuary," a material earthly tabernacle of this world, to be described (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the old worship. The verse marks the ordinances: of divine service. The verse displays the regulation: prescribed rites. The verse displays the sanctuary: worldly. The verse displays the nature: earthly. The order displays the old covenant's regulated earthly worship. The verse displays the service: divine. The verse displays the sanctuary: material. The verse displays the covenant: the first. The first covenant's ordinances and earthly sanctuary display the old order the writer now describes: it had its appointed ordinances of divine worship and a material, earthly sanctuary — a God-given system whose typical meaning, over against the heavenly reality, is now drawn out. The verse displays the earthly sanctuary, detailed in its furniture (vv.2-5).
2:
"For there was a tabernacle made; the first, wherein was the candlestick, and the table, and the shewbread; which is called the sanctuary." The brief verse notes the first part of the tabernacle and its furniture. The grammatical voices note the holy place. Barnes and Robertson note the description of the outer compartment: "there was a tabernacle made; the first (the first compartment, the outer room), wherein was the candlestick (the golden lampstand), and the table, and the shewbread (the bread of the Presence)" — the first room of the tabernacle, containing the lampstand and the table with the showbread.

On "the candlestick... the table... the shewbread," the witnesses note the sacred furniture. Barnes and Calvin note the items: the golden candlestick (lampstand) gave light; the table held the twelve loaves of showbread, set before the Lord; the furniture of the holy place, each with symbolic meaning.

On "which is called the sanctuary," the grammatical-historical note the name: this first room "is called the sanctuary (hagia — the holy place)" — the outer compartment, distinguished from the inner Holy of Holies (v.3); the priests entered here daily. The verse displays the first part of the tabernacle and its furniture.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the outer compartment — "a tabernacle made; the first (outer room), wherein was the candlestick (lampstand), the table, and the shewbread (bread of the Presence)," the first room with its lampstand and table (Barnes, Robertson); the candlestick giving light, the table holding the twelve loaves before the Lord (Barnes, Calvin); "called the sanctuary (the holy place)," the outer compartment the priests entered daily (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the holy place. The verse marks the room: the first. The verse displays the furniture: candlestick, table, shewbread. The verse displays the name: the sanctuary. The verse displays the use: entered daily. The description displays the holy place and its furniture. The verse displays the candlestick: golden. The verse displays the shewbread: set out. The verse displays the sanctuary: the first room. The first part of the tabernacle and its furniture display the old sanctuary's outer compartment: the holy place, containing the golden candlestick, the table, and the showbread — the room the priests entered daily, called the sanctuary. The verse displays the holy place, beyond which lay the Holiest of all (v.3).
3:
"And after the second veil, the tabernacle which is called the Holiest of all;" The brief verse notes the inner tabernacle, the Holiest of all. The grammatical voices note the inner compartment. Barnes and Robertson note the description of the second room: "after the second veil (the inner curtain separating the two compartments), the tabernacle which is called the Holiest of all (hagia hagion — the Holy of Holies)" — beyond the inner veil lay the most sacred inner room.

On "the second veil," the witnesses note the separation. Barnes and Calvin note that "the second veil" was the inner curtain (the first being the entrance screen); it shut off the Holy of Holies from the holy place, marking the boundary none but the high priest might cross.

On "the Holiest of all," the grammatical-historical note the inner sanctuary: "the Holiest of all" — the innermost, most sacred room, where God's presence dwelt above the mercy seat; entered only once a year by the high priest (v.7); the type of heaven itself. The verse displays the inner tabernacle, the Holiest of all.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the inner compartment — "after the second veil (the inner curtain), the tabernacle which is called the Holiest of all (the Holy of Holies)," the most sacred inner room beyond the veil (Barnes, Robertson); "the second veil" the inner curtain shutting off the Holy of Holies, the boundary none but the high priest crossed (Barnes, Calvin); "the Holiest of all," where God's presence dwelt, entered once a year, the type of heaven (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the inner sanctuary. The verse marks the veil: the second. The verse displays the room: the Holiest of all. The verse displays the separation: the inner curtain. The verse displays the type: of heaven. The description displays the most holy inner room. The verse displays the veil: dividing. The verse displays the Holiest: inner. The verse displays the sanctuary: most sacred. The inner tabernacle, the Holiest of all, displays the most sacred part of the old sanctuary: beyond the second veil lay the Holy of Holies — the innermost room where God's presence dwelt, entered only by the high priest once a year, the type of heaven itself. The verse displays the Holy of Holies, with its sacred contents (v.4).
4:
"Which had the golden censer, and the ark of the covenant overlaid round about with gold, wherein was the golden pot that had manna, and Aaron's rod that budded, and the tables of the covenant;" The brief verse notes the sacred contents of the Holy of Holies. The grammatical voices note the furniture. Barnes and Robertson note the items: "the golden censer (or the golden altar of incense), and the ark of the covenant overlaid round about with gold" — the inner sanctuary had the golden censer (some take it as the incense altar) and the ark, the most sacred object, overlaid with gold.

On "the golden pot that had manna, and Aaron's rod that budded, and the tables of the covenant," the witnesses note the contents of the ark. Barnes and Calvin note the three sacred relics within (or associated with) the ark: "the golden pot of manna (the memorial of God's provision), Aaron's rod that budded (the token of God's choice of the priesthood, Numbers 17), and the tables of the covenant (the two stone tablets of the law)" — relics commemorating God's dealings with Israel.

On "the golden censer," interpreters note a difficulty. Barnes and Ellicott note the question whether the "golden censer" was actually kept in the Holy of Holies or was the altar of incense (which stood in the holy place but belonged to the inner service on the Day of Atonement); the writer associates it with the inner room by its function. The verse displays the sacred contents of the Holy of Holies.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the furniture — "the golden censer (or incense altar), and the ark of the covenant overlaid with gold," the inner sanctuary's most sacred objects (Barnes, Robertson); the three relics "the golden pot of manna, Aaron's rod that budded, and the tables of the covenant," commemorating God's dealings (Barnes, Calvin); the "golden censer" raising a question whether kept within or the incense altar associated with the inner service (Barnes, Ellicott). The verse displays the sacred contents. The verse marks the censer: golden. The verse displays the ark: overlaid with gold. The verse displays the relics: manna, rod, tables. The verse displays the memorials: God's dealings. The contents display the most sacred relics of the old covenant. The verse displays the ark: holy. The verse displays the manna: preserved. The verse displays the tables: of the covenant. The sacred contents of the Holy of Holies display the most hallowed objects of the old sanctuary: the golden censer and the gold-overlaid ark of the covenant, containing the pot of manna, Aaron's budded rod, and the tables of the law — relics commemorating God's provision, his choice of the priesthood, and his covenant. The verse displays the ark and its contents, over which the cherubim spread (v.5).
5:
"And over it the cherubims of glory shadowing the mercyseat; of which we cannot now speak particularly." The brief verse notes the cherubim overshadowing the mercy seat. The grammatical voices note the cherubim. Barnes and Robertson note the description above the ark: "over it the cherubims of glory (the glorious cherubim, symbolic winged figures) shadowing (overshadowing, with outspread wings) the mercyseat (hilasterion — the propitiatory, the golden lid of the ark)" — the two cherubim stood over the ark, their wings spread over the mercy seat.

On "the cherubims of glory," the witnesses note their significance. Barnes and Calvin note that the "cherubim of glory" were associated with the visible glory of God (the Shekinah) that dwelt above the mercy seat; they symbolized God's presence and majesty, guarding and adorning the place of his dwelling.

On "the mercyseat... of which we cannot now speak particularly," the grammatical-historical note the propitiatory and the writer's restraint: the "mercyseat" was where the blood was sprinkled on the Day of Atonement, the place of propitiation; but the writer says "we cannot now speak particularly (in detail)" — he forbears to expound each item, hastening to his main point about the two compartments and their meaning. The verse displays the cherubim overshadowing the mercy seat.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the cherubim — "the cherubims of glory (glorious winged figures) shadowing (overshadowing) the mercyseat (the golden lid of the ark)," the two cherubim with wings spread over the mercy seat (Barnes, Robertson); associated with the visible glory of God above the mercy seat, symbolizing his presence (Barnes, Calvin); the "mercyseat" where the blood was sprinkled, the writer forbearing to expound each item in detail (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the mercy seat. The verse marks the cherubim: of glory. The verse displays the overshadowing: the mercy seat. The verse displays the glory: God's presence. The verse displays the restraint: cannot speak particularly. The description displays the cherubim guarding the place of propitiation. The verse displays the cherubim: glorious. The verse displays the mercyseat: overshadowed. The verse displays the speaking: forborne. The cherubim overshadowing the mercy seat display the crown of the old sanctuary's furniture: the cherubim of glory, with outspread wings, overshadowed the mercy seat where the atoning blood was sprinkled and God's glory dwelt — though the writer forbears to expound each item in detail, hastening to his main argument. The verse displays the mercy seat, the focus of the yearly atonement (vv.6-7).
6:
"Now when these things were thus ordained, the priests went always into the first tabernacle, accomplishing the service of God." The brief verse notes the priests' daily service in the first tabernacle. The grammatical voices note the regular ministry. Barnes and Robertson note the routine of the holy place: "when these things were thus ordained (arranged, prepared), the priests went always (continually, daily) into the first tabernacle (the holy place), accomplishing the service of God" — the ordinary priests entered the first compartment daily to perform the regular acts of worship.

On "went always," the witnesses note the constancy. Barnes and Calvin note that the priests entered the holy place "always" — continually, day by day — to trim the lamps, burn incense, and set out the showbread; the regular, repeated service of the outer room.

On "accomplishing the service of God," the puritan Henry notes the worship: they were "accomplishing (performing, carrying out) the service (latreias — the ritual worship) of God" — fulfilling the prescribed daily duties; the constant round of the old covenant's worship in the first tabernacle. The verse displays the priests' daily service in the first tabernacle.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the routine — "the priests went always (daily) into the first tabernacle (the holy place), accomplishing the service of God," the ordinary priests entering the first compartment daily for worship (Barnes, Robertson); entering "always," day by day, to trim lamps, burn incense, set the showbread (Barnes, Calvin); "accomplishing the service of God," performing the prescribed daily duties (Henry). The verse displays the daily service. The verse marks the priests: went always. The verse displays the room: the first tabernacle. The verse displays the constancy: daily. The verse displays the service: of God. The routine displays the priests' constant ministry in the holy place. The verse displays the entering: daily. The verse displays the service: performed. The verse displays the tabernacle: the first. The priests' daily service in the first tabernacle displays the regular worship of the old covenant: the ordinary priests entered the holy place continually, day by day, to perform the prescribed acts of worship — the constant round of the outer room's service. The verse displays the daily ministry, over against the high priest's yearly entrance (v.7).
7:
"But into the second went the high priest alone once every year, not without blood, which he offered for himself, and for the errors of the people:" The witnesses note the high priest's yearly entrance into the Holy of Holies with blood. On "into the second went the high priest alone once every year," the grammatical voices note the restricted access. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast with the daily service: "into the second (the Holy of Holies) went the high priest alone once every year" — only the high priest, and he only once a year (on the Day of Atonement), entered the inner sanctuary; the access was rare and restricted.

On "not without blood," the witnesses note the necessity of blood. Barnes and Calvin note that he entered "not without blood (always with blood)" — he could not enter the Holy of Holies without bringing the blood of sacrifice; blood was indispensable for approaching God's presence.

On "which he offered for himself, and for the errors of the people," the grammatical-historical note the double offering: the blood was offered "for himself (the high priest being a sinner, he had to atone for his own sins first) and for the errors (ignorances, sins of inadvertence) of the people" — the high priest atoned both for his own sins and for the people's; the imperfection of a priest who must offer for himself. The verse displays the high priest's yearly entrance with blood.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the restricted access — "into the second (Holy of Holies) went the high priest alone once every year," only the high priest, once a year on the Day of Atonement (Barnes, Robertson); "not without blood," entering always with the blood of sacrifice, indispensable for approaching God (Barnes, Calvin); "for himself, and for the errors of the people," atoning both for his own sins and the people's, the priest himself a sinner (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the yearly entrance. The verse marks the access: high priest alone, once a year. The verse displays the necessity: not without blood. The verse displays the double offering: for himself and the people. The verse displays the imperfection: the priest a sinner. The entrance displays the high priest atoning with blood yearly. The verse displays the high priest: alone. The verse displays the blood: required. The verse displays the offering: for sin. The high priest's yearly entrance into the Holy of Holies with blood displays the heart of the old atonement: only the high priest, only once a year, entered the inner sanctuary — and never without the blood of sacrifice, offered for his own sins and the people's. The verse displays the restricted access, whose very limits the Spirit used to teach (v.8).
8:
"The Holy Ghost this signifying, that the way into the holiest of all was not yet made manifest, while as the first tabernacle was yet standing:" The witnesses note the Spirit teaching that the way into the holiest was not yet open. On "the Holy Ghost this signifying," the grammatical voices note the divine intent. Barnes and Robertson note that the very arrangement of the tabernacle had a teaching purpose: "the Holy Ghost this signifying (showing, making plain)" — the Spirit, who designed the tabernacle and its rites, intended by them to teach a spiritual lesson; the structure itself was a divine sign.

On "the way into the holiest of all was not yet made manifest," the witnesses note the lesson. Barnes and Calvin note the meaning: "the way into the holiest of all (the access to God's immediate presence) was not yet made manifest (opened, revealed)" — under the old covenant, the way of full access to God was not yet open; the people could not enter, and even the high priest only once a year; the very restriction showed that the way was not yet free.

On "while as the first tabernacle was yet standing," the puritan Henry notes the duration: "while as the first tabernacle was yet standing (while the outer tabernacle, and the whole old system, still stood)" — as long as the old order continued, the way remained closed; only when it gave way (in Christ) would the way be opened. The verse displays the Spirit teaching that the way into the holiest was not yet open.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the divine intent — "the Holy Ghost this signifying (showing)," the Spirit teaching a spiritual lesson by the tabernacle's arrangement (Barnes, Robertson); "the way into the holiest (access to God's presence) was not yet made manifest (opened)," full access not yet open under the old covenant, the restriction itself the sign (Barnes, Calvin); "while as the first tabernacle was yet standing," the way closed as long as the old order continued (Henry). The verse displays the closed way. The verse marks the teacher: the Holy Ghost. The verse displays the lesson: the way not yet open. The verse displays the access: restricted. The verse displays the duration: while the old order stood. The teaching displays the Spirit signifying the closed way. The verse displays the signifying: the Spirit's. The verse displays the way: not manifest. The verse displays the tabernacle: yet standing. The Spirit teaching that the way into the holiest was not yet open displays the typical meaning of the tabernacle's structure: the Holy Ghost, by the very arrangement that barred all but the high priest from the inner room, signified that the way of full access to God was not yet opened — and would not be, as long as the old order stood. The verse displays the not-yet-opened way, a figure of the present age (v.9).
9:
"Which was a figure for the time then present, in which were offered both gifts and sacrifices, that could not make him that did the service perfect, as pertaining to the conscience;" The witnesses note the old offerings unable to perfect the conscience. On "which was a figure for the time then present," the grammatical voices note the symbolism. Barnes and Robertson note that the old tabernacle and its service were "a figure (parabole — a parable, symbol) for the time then present (the present age, the time of the old covenant, or the period until the reformation)" — the whole system was symbolic, pointing beyond itself to a coming reality.

On "in which were offered both gifts and sacrifices," the witnesses note the offerings. Barnes and Calvin note that under this system "were offered both gifts and sacrifices" — the various offerings of the old covenant, continually presented; the regular ritual of the figurative age.

On "that could not make him that did the service perfect, as pertaining to the conscience," the grammatical-historical note the great limitation: these offerings "could not make him that did the service (the worshipper) perfect (teleiosai — complete, fully cleanse), as pertaining to the conscience" — the crucial deficiency: they could not cleanse the conscience or give inward peace; they dealt with ceremonial, outward purity, not with the guilt that troubles the soul. The verse displays the old offerings unable to perfect the conscience.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the symbolism — "a figure (parable, symbol) for the time then present," the whole system symbolic, pointing to a coming reality (Barnes, Robertson); "offered both gifts and sacrifices," the various offerings continually presented (Barnes, Calvin); "could not make him that did the service (the worshipper) perfect, as pertaining to the conscience," unable to cleanse the conscience or give inward peace, dealing with outward not inward purity (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the offerings' limit. The verse marks the figure: for the time present. The verse displays the offerings: gifts and sacrifices. The verse displays the limitation: could not perfect the conscience. The verse displays the deficiency: only outward purity. The figure displays the old offerings unable to cleanse the conscience. The verse displays the parable: the old system. The verse displays the offerings: insufficient. The verse displays the conscience: unperfected. The old offerings unable to perfect the conscience display the crucial deficiency of the figurative system: the tabernacle and its gifts and sacrifices were a symbol for the present age, but they could not make the worshipper perfect as regards the conscience — they could cleanse the flesh ceremonially but not purge the guilt that troubles the soul. The verse displays the conscience unperfected, awaiting the time of reformation (v.10).
10:
"Which stood only in meats and drinks, and divers washings, and carnal ordinances, imposed on them until the time of reformation." The brief verse notes the old ordinances imposed until the time of reformation. The grammatical voices note the external rites. Barnes and Robertson note the nature of the old ordinances: "which stood only in (consisted only of) meats and drinks (regulations about food and drink, clean and unclean), and divers washings (various ceremonial cleansings, baptismois), and carnal ordinances (dikaiomata sarkos — fleshly, external regulations)" — the old system dealt only with outward, physical, ceremonial matters.

On "carnal ordinances," the witnesses note their outward character. Barnes and Calvin note that these were "carnal (fleshly, external) ordinances" — rules about the body and outward purity, not reaching the heart; they could regulate the flesh but not renew the soul.

On "imposed on them until the time of reformation," the grammatical-historical note the temporary character: these were "imposed (laid on as a burden) on them until the time of reformation (diorthosis — the setting right, the new order, the time of the gospel)" — the old ordinances were temporary, binding only until the coming of Christ, who would set all things right; their imposition had an expiry. The verse displays the old ordinances imposed until the time of reformation.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the external rites — "stood only in meats and drinks, and divers washings (ceremonial cleansings), and carnal ordinances (fleshly regulations)," the old system dealing only with outward physical matters (Barnes, Robertson); "carnal ordinances," rules about the body and outward purity, not reaching the heart (Barnes, Calvin); "imposed on them until the time of reformation (the new order, the gospel)," temporary, binding only until Christ set all right (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the temporary ordinances. The verse marks the content: meats, drinks, washings. The verse displays the character: carnal ordinances. The verse displays the imposition: laid as a burden. The verse displays the limit: until the reformation. The ordinances display the outward rites binding only till Christ. The verse displays the meats: regulated. The verse displays the washings: ceremonial. The verse displays the reformation: the expiry. The old ordinances imposed until the time of reformation display the temporary, external character of the old system: it consisted only of regulations about food, drink, and ceremonial washings — fleshly ordinances dealing with outward purity — imposed as a burden only until the coming of Christ, the time of setting all things right. The verse displays the temporary ordinances, surpassed by Christ's coming (v.11).
11:
"But Christ being come an high priest of good things to come, by a greater and more perfect tabernacle, not made with hands, that is to say, not of this building;" The witnesses note Christ come as high priest through the greater heavenly tabernacle. On "Christ being come an high priest of good things to come," the grammatical voices note the new high priest. Barnes and Robertson note the great turn: "Christ being come (having appeared) an high priest of good things to come (the blessings of the new covenant, the good things now realized or yet to come)" — Christ has appeared as high priest, bringing the good things the old system only foreshadowed.

On "by a greater and more perfect tabernacle, not made with hands," the witnesses note the heavenly tabernacle. Barnes and Calvin note that Christ ministers "by (through) a greater and more perfect tabernacle, not made with hands (acheiropoieton — not constructed by human hands)" — through the heavenly sanctuary, greater and more perfect than the earthly, not built by men.

On "not of this building," the eastern Cyril and puritan Henry note the contrast: "not of this building (not of this creation, not belonging to this material world)" — the heavenly tabernacle is not part of the created, material order; interpreters differ whether it means heaven itself, or Christ's own body/humanity, but the point is its heavenly, not earthly, nature. The verse displays Christ come as high priest through the greater heavenly tabernacle.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the new high priest — "Christ being come (appeared) an high priest of good things to come," bringing the blessings the old system foreshadowed (Barnes, Robertson); "by a greater and more perfect tabernacle, not made with hands," ministering through the heavenly sanctuary, greater than the earthly, not built by men (Barnes, Calvin); "not of this building (not of this creation)," not part of the material order, whether heaven itself or Christ's humanity (Cyril, Henry). The verse displays the heavenly tabernacle. The verse marks the priest: Christ come. The verse displays the blessings: good things to come. The verse displays the tabernacle: greater, not made with hands. The verse displays the nature: not of this building. The coming displays Christ ministering through the heavenly sanctuary. The verse displays the high priest: come. The verse displays the tabernacle: heavenly. The verse displays the building: not earthly. Christ come as high priest through the greater heavenly tabernacle displays the great contrast with the old: Christ has appeared as high priest of the good things now come, ministering through a greater and more perfect tabernacle — not made with hands, not of this material creation, but heavenly. The verse displays the heavenly high priest, who entered with his own blood (v.12).
12:
"Neither by the blood of goats and calves, but by his own blood he entered in once into the holy place, having obtained eternal redemption for us." The witnesses note Christ entering the holy place once by his own blood, obtaining eternal redemption. On "neither by the blood of goats and calves, but by his own blood," the grammatical voices note the better blood. Barnes and Robertson note the great contrast: "neither by (with) the blood of goats and calves (the animal sacrifices of the old covenant), but by his own blood" — Christ entered the heavenly sanctuary not with the blood of animals but with his own blood; the infinitely superior sacrifice.

On "he entered in once into the holy place," the witnesses note the once-for-all entrance. Barnes and Calvin note the key word: he "entered in once (hapax — once for all, not yearly) into the holy place (the heavenly Holy of Holies)" — unlike the high priest's yearly entrance, Christ entered once for all, his single entrance sufficient and final.

On "having obtained eternal redemption for us," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the result: "having obtained (secured, found) eternal redemption (lytrosis — ransoming, deliverance) for us" — by his one entrance with his own blood, Christ secured an eternal redemption; not a yearly, repeated atonement but a redemption that is everlasting and complete. The verse displays Christ entering once by his own blood, obtaining eternal redemption.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the better blood — "neither by the blood of goats and calves, but by his own blood," Christ entering with his own blood, not animals', the superior sacrifice (Barnes, Robertson); "he entered in once (once for all) into the holy place (the heavenly sanctuary)," a single, final entrance unlike the yearly (Barnes, Calvin); "having obtained eternal redemption for us," securing an everlasting, complete redemption (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays eternal redemption. The verse marks the blood: his own. The verse displays the entrance: once into the holy place. The verse displays the contrast: not animal blood. The verse displays the result: eternal redemption. The entrance displays Christ securing eternal redemption by his blood. The verse displays the blood: his own. The verse displays the once: for all. The verse displays the redemption: eternal. Christ entering the holy place once by his own blood, obtaining eternal redemption, displays the crown of his priestly work: he entered the heavenly sanctuary not with the blood of goats and calves but with his own blood — once for all — and by that single offering secured an eternal redemption for us. The verse displays the eternal redemption, far surpassing the cleansing blood of animals (vv.13-14).
13:
"For if the blood of bulls and of goats, and the ashes of an heifer sprinkling the unclean, sanctifieth to the purifying of the flesh:" The brief verse notes the old sacrifices cleansing only the flesh. The grammatical voices note the limited efficacy. Barnes and Robertson note the premise of the argument from lesser to greater: "if the blood of bulls and of goats, and the ashes of an heifer (the red heifer, Numbers 19) sprinkling the unclean, sanctifieth (sets apart, cleanses ceremonially)" — the old sacrifices and the water of purification did have a real effect.

On "the ashes of an heifer sprinkling the unclean," the witnesses note the ceremonial cleansing. Barnes and Calvin note the rite (Numbers 19): the ashes of the red heifer, mixed with water and sprinkled on those defiled (by contact with death), removed ceremonial uncleanness; a typical purification.

On "sanctifieth to the purifying of the flesh," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the limit: these rites "sanctifieth to the purifying of the flesh (the outward, bodily, ceremonial cleansing)" — they cleansed only outwardly, restoring ceremonial purity so the person could rejoin worship; they reached the flesh, not the conscience; their efficacy was real but external and limited. The verse displays the old sacrifices cleansing only the flesh.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the premise — "if the blood of bulls and goats, and the ashes of an heifer (Numbers 19) sprinkling the unclean, sanctifieth (cleanses ceremonially)," the old sacrifices having a real effect (Barnes, Robertson); the red heifer's ashes removing ceremonial uncleanness from contact with death, a typical purification (Barnes, Calvin); "to the purifying of the flesh," cleansing only outwardly, reaching the flesh not the conscience, real but external (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the outward cleansing. The verse marks the rites: blood and ashes. The verse displays the effect: sanctifies. The verse displays the limit: the flesh only. The verse displays the contrast: not the conscience. The premise displays the old rites cleansing outwardly. The verse displays the blood: of bulls and goats. The verse displays the ashes: of a heifer. The verse displays the purifying: of the flesh. The old sacrifices cleansing only the flesh display the premise of the argument from lesser to greater: the blood of bulls and goats and the ashes of the red heifer did cleanse the ceremonially unclean — but only outwardly, purifying the flesh, not reaching the conscience. The verse displays the outward cleansing, far surpassed by the blood of Christ (v.14).
14:
"How much more shall the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without spot to God, purge your conscience from dead works to serve the living God?" The witnesses note the blood of Christ cleansing the conscience — with the crux of the eternal Spirit. On "how much more shall the blood of Christ," the grammatical voices note the argument. Barnes and Robertson note the a fortiori reasoning: "how much more (by how much the superior) shall the blood of Christ" — if animal blood cleansed the flesh, how much more shall Christ's infinitely precious blood cleanse the soul; the blood of the Son of God surpasses all.

On "who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without spot to God," interpreters divide over "the eternal Spirit." Barnes (preferring it), with Wesley and Calvin, take "the eternal Spirit" as the Holy Spirit — Christ offered himself under the influences of the Eternal Spirit; the great mass of readers and the technical sense of "the Spirit" favour this. Robertson and Ellicott take it as Christ's own divine nature/eternal spirit — "not the Holy Spirit, but Christ's own spirit which is eternal as he is," the higher principle of his being that gave his offering infinite worth. A third view (Grotius, noted by Calvin) takes it as "endless life" (like 7:16). All agree Christ "offered himself without spot (amomon — without blemish, the spotless victim) to God" — the sinless self-offering.

On "purge your conscience from dead works to serve the living God," the grammatical-historical note the inward cleansing: Christ's blood will "purge (cleanse) your conscience from dead works (sinful, lifeless deeds) to serve the living God" — what the old sacrifices could not do, Christ's blood does: it cleanses the conscience itself, removing the guilt that troubles the soul, freeing the believer to serve God. The verse displays the blood of Christ cleansing the conscience.

The witnesses agree the substance: the blood of Christ, the spotless self-offering, infinitely surpasses animal blood and cleanses the conscience from dead works, freeing the believer to serve the living God — the inward cleansing the old sacrifices could not give (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin). But "the eternal Spirit" divides them: Barnes, Wesley, and Calvin take it as the Holy Spirit, under whose influences Christ offered himself; Robertson and Ellicott as Christ's own divine, eternal spirit/nature; Grotius (noted) as endless life. The verse displays the cleansing blood and the crux. The verse marks the blood: of Christ. The verse displays the debate: the Holy Spirit, Christ's own spirit, or endless life. The verse displays the offering: himself, without spot. The verse displays the effect: purge the conscience. The blood displays Christ cleansing the soul to serve God. The verse displays the blood: precious. The verse displays the conscience: purged. The verse displays the service: of the living God. The blood of Christ cleansing the conscience displays the crown of the argument and a crux: how much more shall the blood of Christ — who through the eternal Spirit (the Holy Spirit, or his own divine nature) offered himself without spot to God — purge the conscience from dead works, that the believer may serve the living God. The verse displays the conscience-cleansing blood, making Christ the mediator of the new covenant (v.15).
15:
"And for this cause he is the mediator of the new testament, that by means of death, for the redemption of the transgressions that were under the first testament, they which are called might receive the promise of eternal inheritance." The witnesses note Christ the mediator of the new covenant by means of death. On "he is the mediator of the new testament," the grammatical voices note the mediatorship. Barnes and Robertson note that Christ's cleansing blood (v.14) qualifies him: "for this cause he is the mediator (go-between, reconciler) of the new testament (diatheke — covenant)" — Christ stands between God and man as mediator of the new covenant, securing its blessings.

On "by means of death, for the redemption of the transgressions that were under the first testament," the witnesses note the retrospective efficacy. Barnes and Calvin note that "by means of death (his own death), for the redemption (ransoming, deliverance) of the transgressions that were under the first testament" — Christ's death redeems even the sins committed under the old covenant; his atonement reaches back, covering the transgressions the old sacrifices could not truly take away.

On "they which are called might receive the promise of eternal inheritance," the eastern Chrysostom and grammatical-historical note the purpose: "that they which are called (the elect, those summoned by the gospel) might receive the promise of eternal inheritance" — the end of Christ's mediation and death is that the called may obtain the promised eternal inheritance; the everlasting reward the old covenant could only foreshadow. The verse displays Christ the mediator of the new covenant by means of death.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the mediatorship — "he is the mediator (reconciler) of the new testament (covenant)," Christ standing between God and man, securing the covenant's blessings (Barnes, Robertson); "by means of death, for the redemption of the transgressions under the first testament," his death redeeming even the sins of the old covenant, reaching back (Barnes, Calvin); "they which are called might receive the promise of eternal inheritance," the called obtaining the promised eternal reward (Chrysostom, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the mediator. The verse marks the office: mediator of the new covenant. The verse displays the means: his death. The verse displays the redemption: of past transgressions. The verse displays the goal: eternal inheritance. The mediation displays Christ securing the inheritance by his death. The verse displays the mediator: Christ. The verse displays the death: the means. The verse displays the inheritance: eternal. Christ the mediator of the new covenant by means of death displays the fruit of his atoning blood: he is the mediator of the new covenant, whose death redeems even the transgressions committed under the old — that the called might receive the promised eternal inheritance. The verse displays the mediating death, whose necessity the figure of a testament illustrates (vv.16-17).
16:
"For where a testament is, there must also of necessity be the death of the testator." The witnesses note the principle that a testament requires the death — the crux of covenant or testament. On the meaning of "testament," interpreters divide. The received reading (the AV) takes diatheke here as "testament" (a will): "where a testament (a will) is, there must of necessity be the death of the testator (the one who made the will)" — a will takes effect only when the maker dies; so Christ's death was necessary to make his testament effective. Gill follows this (the covenant of grace is a will/testament), and Robertson notes the word plays on both senses ("we say today The New Testament").

But the grammatical-historical Barnes, Ellicott, with Clarke and Calvin, hold the word should be rendered "covenant" throughout (consistent with vv.15, 18, the OT berith): "where a covenant is, there must be the death of the covenanter (or of the appointed victim)" — a covenant with God was ratified by the death of a sacrificial victim; the "death of the testator" being the death of the victim slain to confirm the covenant. Barnes argues at length that diatheke never means "will" elsewhere in Scripture, and that the argument concerns covenant-ratification by sacrifice, not a legal will; Clarke: "we should endeavour to forget that testament and testator were ever introduced, as they totally change the apostle's meaning"; Ellicott holds the difficulties of the "will" reading "beyond comparison more serious." Wesley too finds it "beneath the dignity of the apostle to play upon the ambiguity."

The witnesses note the necessity of death either way: whether a will requiring the maker's death or a covenant ratified by a victim's death, the point is that death was necessary — and so Christ had to die. The verse displays the principle that a testament requires the death.

The witnesses agree the conclusion: death was necessary to make the covenant (or testament) effective, and so Christ had to die (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Gill). But the rendering divides them: the AV and Gill (with Robertson noting the double sense) take diatheke as "testament/will," requiring the death of the testator; Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott, and Calvin render "covenant," the death being that of the sacrificial victim/covenanter that ratifies it, holding the "will" reading foreign to the epistle's Jewish argument. The verse displays the diatheke crux. The verse marks the principle: death required. The verse displays the debate: testament/will, or covenant/victim. The verse displays the necessity: the death. The verse displays the application: Christ's death. The principle displays death necessary to ratify the covenant or testament. The verse displays the testament: or covenant. The verse displays the death: required. The verse displays the testator: or victim. The principle that a testament requires the death displays a famous translation crux: "where a testament (or covenant) is, there must be the death of the testator (or the appointed victim)" — read as a will taking effect at the maker's death (AV, Gill, Robertson) or a covenant ratified by a sacrificial victim's death (Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott, Calvin) — the point being that death was necessary, and so Christ died. The verse displays the necessary death, since even the first covenant was ratified with blood (vv.18-22).
17:
"For a testament is of force after men are dead: otherwise it is of no strength at all while the testator liveth." The brief verse notes that the testament takes force only by death. The grammatical voices note the principle continued. Barnes and Robertson note the explanation of v.16: "a testament is of force (valid, ratified) after men are dead (or, over the dead — epi nekrois): otherwise it is of no strength at all while the testator liveth" — whether of a will (valid only at the maker's death) or a covenant (ratified over the dead sacrificial victim), it has no force until death intervenes.

On "after men are dead," interpreters note the same crux as v.16. Barnes (and Clarke) hold "over the dead" refers to the slain sacrificial victims that ratify the covenant — the covenant is confirmed "over dead" sacrifices, not valid while the victim lives; Barnes notes "dead (nekrois)" need not mean only dead men but can include dead animals (cf. "dead works," 6:1; 9:14). The AV/Gill reading takes it of a human will, valid only after the maker dies.

On "no strength while the testator liveth," the grammatical-historical note the conclusion: the covenant/testament "is of no strength while the testator (or victim) liveth" — death is indispensable to its validity; this confirms that Christ's death was necessary to ratify the new covenant. The verse displays that the testament takes force only by death.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the principle — "a testament is of force after men are dead (over the dead): otherwise of no strength while the testator liveth," no force until death intervenes (Barnes, Robertson); "over the dead" referring to the slain victims ratifying the covenant (Barnes, Clarke), or to a human will valid only at the maker's death (AV, Gill); death indispensable to validity, confirming Christ's death necessary (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the force by death. The verse marks the validity: after death. The verse displays the figure: over the dead. The verse displays the necessity: death required. The verse displays the application: Christ's death. The principle displays the covenant/testament valid only by death. The verse displays the testament: of force. The verse displays the death: required. The verse displays the strength: none without it. That the testament takes force only by death displays the confirmation of the principle: a testament (or covenant) is valid only after death — over the dead, whether the maker of a will or the sacrificial victim — having no force while the testator (or victim) yet lives; so Christ's death was necessary. The verse displays the death that ratifies, as the first covenant too was dedicated with blood (v.18).
18:
"Whereupon neither the first testament was dedicated without blood." The brief verse notes that the first covenant too was dedicated with blood. The grammatical voices note the proof from history. Barnes and Robertson note the application: "whereupon (for which reason, in keeping with the principle that death ratifies) neither the first testament (covenant) was dedicated (inaugurated, ratified) without blood" — even the old covenant was not established without blood; its very ratification required death.

On "the first testament," the witnesses note the old covenant. Barnes and Calvin note that "the first testament (covenant)" — the Sinai covenant — was confirmed with blood at its inauguration (Exodus 24), proving that blood (and so death) was necessary to ratify even the old covenant.

On "not dedicated without blood," the grammatical-historical note the principle confirmed: the old covenant was "not dedicated without blood" — its very establishment required the shedding of blood; this historical fact (detailed in vv.19-21) proves that the principle of v.16-17 (death ratifies a covenant) held even for the old covenant, and so applies to the new. The verse displays that the first covenant too was dedicated with blood.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the application — "neither the first testament (covenant) was dedicated (ratified) without blood," even the old covenant established with blood (Barnes, Robertson); "the first testament" the Sinai covenant, confirmed with blood at Exodus 24, blood necessary to ratify it (Barnes, Calvin); "not dedicated without blood," its establishment requiring the shedding of blood, proving the principle (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the blood-dedication. The verse marks the covenant: the first. The verse displays the dedication: not without blood. The verse displays the proof: from Sinai. The verse displays the principle: death ratifies. The application displays even the old covenant ratified by blood. The verse displays the testament: first. The verse displays the blood: required. The verse displays the dedication: confirmed. That the first covenant too was dedicated with blood displays the historical confirmation of the principle: even the old covenant at Sinai was not established without blood — its very ratification required the shedding of blood, proving that death is necessary to confirm a covenant, and so that Christ's death ratifies the new. The verse displays the blood-dedicated covenant, sprinkled by Moses on book and people (vv.19-20).
19:
"For when Moses had spoken every precept to all the people according to the law, he took the blood of calves and of goats, with water, and scarlet wool, and hyssop, and sprinkled both the book, and all the people," The witnesses note Moses ratifying the old covenant by sprinkling blood. On "when Moses had spoken every precept to all the people," the grammatical voices note the covenant-making. Barnes and Robertson note the historical account (Exodus 24): "when Moses had spoken every precept (commandment) to all the people according to the law" — after declaring the law, Moses proceeded to ratify the covenant.

On "he took the blood of calves and of goats, with water, and scarlet wool, and hyssop," the witnesses note the rite. Barnes and Cyril note the materials of the ratification: "the blood of calves and of goats, with water, and scarlet wool, and hyssop" — the blood (the means of ratification), mixed and applied with the prescribed materials; the writer adds details (water, wool, hyssop, goats) drawn from the fuller priestly tradition.

On "sprinkled both the book, and all the people," the grammatical-historical note the application: Moses "sprinkled both the book (the book of the covenant), and all the people" — the blood was sprinkled on the covenant document and on the people; both the covenant and the people were consecrated by blood, sealing the agreement. The verse displays Moses ratifying the old covenant by sprinkling blood.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the covenant-making (Exodus 24) — "when Moses had spoken every precept to all the people," declaring the law before ratifying (Barnes, Robertson); "the blood of calves and goats, with water, scarlet wool, and hyssop," the materials of ratification, with details from priestly tradition (Barnes, Cyril); "sprinkled both the book, and all the people," blood on the covenant document and the people, consecrating both (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the blood-sprinkling. The verse marks the precepts: spoken. The verse displays the blood: of calves and goats. The verse displays the materials: water, wool, hyssop. The verse displays the sprinkling: book and people. The rite displays Moses ratifying the covenant with blood. The verse displays the Moses: ratifying. The verse displays the blood: sprinkled. The verse displays the people: consecrated. Moses ratifying the old covenant by sprinkling blood displays the historical proof: after declaring the law, Moses took the blood of calves and goats, with water, scarlet wool, and hyssop, and sprinkled both the book of the covenant and all the people — sealing the old covenant with blood. The verse displays the blood-sealed covenant, with the solemn words of dedication (v.20).
20:
"Saying, This is the blood of the testament which God hath enjoined unto you." The brief verse notes Moses' words declaring the blood of the covenant. The grammatical voices note the citation. Barnes and Robertson note Moses' solemn declaration (Exodus 24:8): "This is the blood of the testament (covenant) which God hath enjoined (commanded, ordained) unto you" — Moses, sprinkling the blood, pronounced it the blood that ratified the covenant God had commanded.

On "the blood of the testament," the witnesses note the covenant-blood. Barnes and Calvin note that the blood was "the blood of the covenant" — the blood by which the covenant was sealed and made binding; the people were bound to God by this blood.

On the parallel with Christ, the grammatical-historical note the echo: Moses' words "This is the blood of the covenant" are strikingly echoed by Christ at the Last Supper — "This is my blood of the new testament" (Matthew 26:28); the old covenant's blood-ratification foreshadows the new covenant sealed in Christ's blood. The verse displays Moses' words declaring the blood of the covenant.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the citation (Exodus 24:8) — "This is the blood of the testament (covenant) which God hath enjoined (commanded) unto you," Moses pronouncing the blood that ratified the covenant (Barnes, Robertson); "the blood of the covenant," the blood sealing and binding the covenant (Barnes, Calvin); Moses' words echoed by Christ at the Supper ("This is my blood of the new testament," Matthew 26:28), the old foreshadowing the new (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the covenant-blood. The verse marks the declaration: this is the blood. The verse displays the covenant: ratified. The verse displays the command: God enjoined. The verse displays the echo: Christ's words. The declaration displays the blood sealing the covenant. The verse displays the blood: of the covenant. The verse displays the enjoining: God's. The verse displays the sealing: by blood. Moses' words declaring the blood of the covenant display the solemn ratification: sprinkling the blood, Moses pronounced it "the blood of the covenant which God hath enjoined unto you" — words strikingly echoed by Christ over the cup, the old covenant's blood foreshadowing the new sealed in his own. The verse displays the covenant-blood, with which the sanctuary too was sprinkled (v.21).
21:
"Moreover he sprinkled with blood both the tabernacle, and all the vessels of the ministry." The brief verse notes the tabernacle and vessels also sprinkled with blood. The grammatical voices note the further sprinkling. Barnes and Robertson note that beyond the book and people (v.19), Moses (or Aaron) "sprinkled with blood both the tabernacle, and all the vessels of the ministry" — the sacred tent and all the implements of worship were also consecrated by blood; everything connected with the covenant worship was sanctified by sprinkling.

On "the tabernacle," the witnesses note the sanctuary. Barnes and Ellicott note that the tabernacle itself, the place of worship, was sprinkled with blood — purified and dedicated; the writer draws on the broader account of the consecration of the sanctuary and its furniture.

On "all the vessels of the ministry," the grammatical-historical note the implements: "all the vessels of the ministry (the instruments used in the service)" — the altar, basins, and other furniture were likewise sprinkled; the pervasiveness of blood in the old worship — everything cleansed and consecrated by it — proving its necessity. The verse displays the tabernacle and vessels sprinkled with blood.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the further sprinkling — "he sprinkled with blood both the tabernacle, and all the vessels of the ministry," the sacred tent and worship implements consecrated by blood (Barnes, Robertson); "the tabernacle" itself purified and dedicated by sprinkling (Barnes, Ellicott); "all the vessels of the ministry," the altar and furniture likewise sprinkled, blood pervading the old worship (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the sprinkled sanctuary. The verse marks the tabernacle: sprinkled. The verse displays the vessels: of the ministry. The verse displays the consecration: by blood. The verse displays the pervasiveness: everything sprinkled. The sprinkling displays the whole sanctuary consecrated by blood. The verse displays the tabernacle: cleansed. The verse displays the vessels: dedicated. The verse displays the blood: pervasive. The tabernacle and vessels also sprinkled with blood display the pervasiveness of blood in the old worship: not only the book and people, but the tabernacle itself and all the implements of ministry were sprinkled with blood — everything consecrated by it, proving how essential blood was to the whole old covenant order. The verse displays the blood-consecrated sanctuary, for almost all is purged by blood (v.22).
22:
"And almost all things are by the law purged with blood; and without shedding of blood is no remission." The witnesses note the law's rule: almost all purged by blood, no remission without bloodshed. On "almost all things are by the law purged with blood," the grammatical voices note the general principle. Barnes and Robertson note the summary: "almost (schedon — nearly, with few exceptions) all things are by the law purged (cleansed, purified) with blood" — under the law, nearly everything was cleansed by blood; blood was the great means of purification (the "almost" allowing for exceptions, e.g., cleansing by water or fire, Numbers 31:23, or the poor's offering of flour, Leviticus 5:11).

On "without shedding of blood is no remission," the witnesses note the crucial principle. Barnes and Calvin note the great maxim: "without shedding of blood (haimatekchysia) is no remission (aphesis — forgiveness, putting away of sin)" — there is no forgiveness of sin without the shedding of blood; blood, representing life given up in death, is the necessary price of pardon; the foundational principle of the whole sacrificial system.

On the bearing, the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the application to Christ: this principle — no remission without bloodshed — points to the necessity of Christ's death; if even the old covenant required blood for forgiveness, then the true remission of sins required the shedding of Christ's blood. The verse displays the law's rule: almost all purged by blood, no remission without bloodshed.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the general principle — "almost (nearly, with few exceptions) all things are by the law purged (cleansed) with blood," blood the great means of purification, the "almost" allowing exceptions (Barnes, Robertson); the great maxim "without shedding of blood is no remission (forgiveness)," no pardon without blood, life given up in death the price (Barnes, Calvin); the principle pointing to the necessity of Christ's death for true remission (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the rule of blood. The verse marks the purifying: almost all by blood. The verse displays the maxim: no remission without bloodshed. The verse displays the necessity: blood for pardon. The verse displays the application: Christ's death. The rule displays forgiveness requiring the shedding of blood. The verse displays the purging: by blood. The verse displays the remission: none without blood. The verse displays the bloodshed: required. The law's rule — almost all purged by blood, no remission without bloodshed — displays the foundational principle of atonement: under the law nearly everything was cleansed with blood, and there is no forgiveness of sin without the shedding of blood — a principle that demanded the death of Christ for the true remission of sins. The verse displays the necessity of bloodshed, requiring better sacrifices for the heavenly things (v.23).
23:
"It was therefore necessary that the patterns of things in the heavens should be purified with these; but the heavenly things themselves with better sacrifices than these." The witnesses note the heavenly things purified with better sacrifices. On "the patterns of things in the heavens should be purified with these," the grammatical voices note the earthly copies. Barnes and Robertson note the argument: "it was necessary that the patterns (copies, the earthly tabernacle and its furniture, copies of heavenly realities) of things in the heavens should be purified with these (the animal blood)" — the earthly copies were cleansed with animal sacrifices.

On "the heavenly things themselves with better sacrifices," the witnesses note the better cleansing. Barnes and Calvin note the contrast: "but the heavenly things themselves (the realities the earthly copied) with better sacrifices than these" — the heavenly realities required a better sacrifice; if the copies needed blood, the heavenly things needed the better blood of Christ.

On "the heavenly things... purified," interpreters note a difficulty. Barnes and Henry note the question how "heavenly things" could need "purifying" — most take it not that heaven itself was defiled, but that the heavenly sanctuary is inaugurated and the way into it consecrated by Christ's blood; or that the people who enter heaven are purified; the "purifying" is the consecration or opening of the heavenly things by a better sacrifice. The verse displays the heavenly things purified with better sacrifices.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the earthly copies — "the patterns (copies) of things in the heavens should be purified with these (animal blood)," the earthly tabernacle cleansed with animal sacrifices (Barnes, Robertson); "the heavenly things themselves with better sacrifices," the heavenly realities requiring the better blood of Christ (Barnes, Calvin); the "purifying" of heavenly things being their consecration or the opening of the way by Christ's blood, not that heaven was defiled (Barnes, Henry). The verse displays the better sacrifice. The verse marks the copies: purified with animal blood. The verse displays the heavenly: better sacrifices. The verse displays the contrast: copies and realities. The verse displays the difficulty: how heaven is purified. The argument displays the heavenly things consecrated by Christ's blood. The verse displays the patterns: cleansed. The verse displays the heavenly: better blood. The verse displays the sacrifices: superior. The heavenly things purified with better sacrifices display the climax of the argument from lesser to greater: if the earthly copies of heavenly realities were cleansed with animal blood, the heavenly things themselves required better sacrifices — the blood of Christ, by which the heavenly sanctuary is consecrated and its way opened. The verse displays the better sacrifice, offered by Christ in heaven itself (v.24).
24:
"For Christ is not entered into the holy places made with hands, which are the figures of the true; but into heaven itself, now to appear in the presence of God for us:" The witnesses note Christ entered heaven itself to appear before God for us. On "Christ is not entered into the holy places made with hands," the grammatical voices note the contrast. Barnes and Robertson note that Christ's sanctuary is not the earthly: "Christ is not entered into the holy places made with hands (the earthly tabernacle, built by men), which are the figures (antitypes, copies) of the true (the real, heavenly sanctuary)" — Christ did not enter the earthly Holy of Holies, a mere copy.

On "but into heaven itself," the witnesses note the true sanctuary. Barnes and Calvin note that Christ entered "into heaven itself" — the true sanctuary, the very presence of God, of which the earthly was only a figure; his priestly ministry is in heaven.

On "now to appear in the presence of God for us," the eastern Chrysostom and grammatical-historical note the intercession: Christ entered "now to appear (be manifested, present himself) in the presence of God (before God's face) for us (on our behalf)" — Christ now appears before God for his people, presenting himself and his finished sacrifice, ever interceding; his entrance into heaven is for our sake. The verse displays Christ entered heaven itself to appear before God for us.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the contrast — "Christ is not entered into the holy places made with hands (the earthly tabernacle), which are the figures (copies) of the true," not the earthly Holy of Holies (Barnes, Robertson); "but into heaven itself," the true sanctuary, God's very presence (Barnes, Calvin); "now to appear in the presence of God for us," appearing before God for his people, presenting his sacrifice, interceding (Chrysostom, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the heavenly entrance. The verse marks the negative: not the earthly. The verse displays the figures: copies of the true. The verse displays the true: heaven itself. The verse displays the purpose: appear for us. The entrance displays Christ before God on our behalf. The verse displays the holy places: not earthly. The verse displays the heaven: entered. The verse displays the appearing: for us. Christ entered heaven itself to appear before God for us displays the surpassing place of his ministry: he entered not the earthly sanctuary made with hands — a mere copy of the true — but heaven itself, now to appear in the very presence of God on our behalf, presenting his finished sacrifice. The verse displays the heavenly intercession, needing no repeated offering (v.25).
25:
"Nor yet that he should offer himself often, as the high priest entereth into the holy place every year with blood of others;" The brief verse notes that Christ did not offer himself repeatedly like the high priest. The grammatical voices note the contrast of repetition. Barnes and Robertson note the difference: "nor yet that he should offer himself often (repeatedly), as the high priest entereth into the holy place every year with blood of others (the blood of animals, not his own)" — Christ did not, like the Levitical high priest, offer repeatedly year by year.

On "as the high priest entereth... every year," the witnesses note the yearly repetition. Barnes and Calvin note that the high priest entered the Holy of Holies "every year" with fresh blood — a repeated, never-finished offering; the repetition proved its insufficiency.

On "with blood of others," the grammatical-historical note the contrast of the sacrifice: the high priest entered "with blood of others (allotrio — of another, the blood of animals)" — not his own blood; whereas Christ offered his own blood, once. The double contrast — repeated vs once, others' blood vs his own — sets Christ's offering infinitely above the Levitical. The verse displays that Christ did not offer himself repeatedly.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the difference — "nor yet that he should offer himself often (repeatedly), as the high priest entereth... every year with blood of others (animals')," Christ not offering repeatedly like the Levitical priest (Barnes, Robertson); the high priest entering "every year" with fresh blood, a never-finished offering proving insufficiency (Barnes, Calvin); "with blood of others (animals', not his own)," whereas Christ offered his own blood once, the double contrast (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the non-repetition. The verse marks the negative: not offer often. The verse displays the high priest: yearly. The verse displays the blood: of others. The verse displays the contrast: repeated, animal blood. The contrast displays Christ's single self-offering above the repeated. The verse displays the offering: not repeated. The verse displays the yearly: the Levitical. The verse displays the blood: others'. That Christ did not offer himself repeatedly like the high priest displays the finality of his sacrifice: unlike the Levitical high priest who entered the Holy of Holies every year with the blood of animals, Christ did not offer himself again and again — his offering, of his own blood, was once for all. The verse displays the unrepeated offering, since repetition would mean repeated suffering (v.26).
26:
"For then must he often have suffered since the foundation of the world: but now once in the end of the world hath he appeared to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself." The witnesses note Christ appearing once in the end of the world to put away sin. On "then must he often have suffered since the foundation of the world," the grammatical voices note the argument against repetition. Barnes and Robertson note the reasoning: "for then (if he had to offer repeatedly) must he often have suffered since the foundation of the world" — since Christ's offering was the offering of himself in death, repeated offering would require repeated suffering and dying; an absurdity, since his death cannot be repeated.

On "but now once in the end of the world hath he appeared," the witnesses note the once-for-all manifestation. Barnes and Calvin note the contrast: "but now once (hapax — once for all) in the end of the world (the consummation of the ages, the close of the old order) hath he appeared (been manifested)" — Christ appeared a single time, at the climax of history, to accomplish what needed doing only once.

On "to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the purpose: he appeared "to put away (abolish, annul, athetesis) sin by the sacrifice of himself" — to do away with sin, to abolish its guilt and power, by offering himself; the single self-sacrifice accomplished the final putting-away of sin that the repeated animal offerings never could. The verse displays Christ appearing once to put away sin.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the argument — "then must he often have suffered since the foundation of the world," repeated offering requiring repeated suffering, an absurdity since his death cannot be repeated (Barnes, Robertson); "now once (once for all) in the end of the world (the consummation of the ages) hath he appeared," a single manifestation at history's climax (Barnes, Calvin); "to put away (abolish) sin by the sacrifice of himself," doing away with sin by his single self-sacrifice (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the final sacrifice. The verse marks the absurdity: often have suffered. The verse displays the manifestation: once, in the end. The verse displays the purpose: put away sin. The verse displays the means: the sacrifice of himself. The appearing displays Christ abolishing sin by one offering. The verse displays the suffering: not repeated. The verse displays the appearing: once. The verse displays the sin: put away. Christ appearing once in the end of the world to put away sin displays the finality of his work: repeated offering would mean repeated suffering since the world began — but instead, once for all at the consummation of the ages, Christ appeared to put away sin by the single sacrifice of himself. The verse displays the once-for-all sacrifice, answering the one death and judgment of men (vv.27-28).
27:
"And as it is appointed unto men once to die, but after this the judgment:" The brief verse notes that men die once and then face judgment. The grammatical voices note the human lot. Barnes and Robertson note the universal appointment: "as it is appointed (set, ordained by God) unto men once to die" — death comes to every man once; it is the fixed, divine appointment, the universal lot of mankind.

On "once to die," the witnesses note the singleness. Barnes and Calvin note the emphasis on "once" — men die a single time; this against any notion of repeated deaths, and forming the parallel with Christ's single death (v.28); each man dies once, not repeatedly.

On "but after this the judgment," the grammatical-historical note the sequel: "after this (after death) the judgment" — death is followed by judgment; the soul, having died once, comes to account before God; the certainty of judgment after death (against any idea of a second probation or escape). The verse displays that men die once and then face judgment.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the universal appointment — "as it is appointed (ordained by God) unto men once to die," death the fixed lot of every man, a single time (Barnes, Robertson); "once" emphasizing the single death, paralleling Christ's (Barnes, Calvin); "after this the judgment," death followed by judgment, the soul accounting before God (grammatical-historical). The verse displays death and judgment. The verse marks the appointment: once to die. The verse displays the certainty: every man. The verse displays the sequel: the judgment. The verse displays the order: death then judgment. The lot displays men dying once and judged. The verse displays the dying: once. The verse displays the judgment: after. The verse displays the appointment: God's. That men die once and then face judgment displays the human lot that parallels Christ's work: it is appointed to every man to die a single time, and after death comes the judgment — the fixed, certain sequence of human destiny. The verse displays the one death and judgment, mirrored in Christ's one offering and second coming (v.28).
28:
"So Christ was once offered to bear the sins of many; and unto them that look for him shall he appear the second time without sin unto salvation." The witnesses note Christ once offered for sins, appearing a second time for salvation. On "Christ was once offered to bear the sins of many," the grammatical voices note the parallel and the sin-bearing. Barnes and Robertson note the correspondence with v.27: "so (in like manner) Christ was once (once for all, answering to man's once dying) offered to bear (anenenkein — to carry, take up and away) the sins of many" — as men die once, so Christ was offered once, bearing the sins of many (the multitude of the redeemed, cf. Isaiah 53:12).

On "to bear the sins of many," the witnesses note the substitution. Barnes and Calvin note that Christ was offered "to bear the sins of many" — to take upon himself and carry away the sins of his people; the language of substitution, Christ bearing in his own person the sins of the many he came to save.

On "unto them that look for him shall he appear the second time without sin unto salvation," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the second coming: as the high priest, having offered, came out of the sanctuary to bless the waiting people, so Christ "shall appear the second time" — not now to deal with sin (that being finished), but "without sin (apart from sin, no longer bearing it) unto salvation" — to bring the full, final salvation; and this "unto them that look for him (eagerly await him)," the believers who watch for his return. The verse displays Christ once offered, appearing a second time for salvation.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the parallel — "so Christ was once (once for all, answering man's once dying) offered to bear (carry away) the sins of many (the redeemed, Isaiah 53:12)," Christ offered once, bearing sins substitutionarily (Barnes, Robertson); "to bear the sins of many," taking on himself and carrying away his people's sins (Barnes, Calvin); "shall he appear the second time without sin (no longer bearing sin) unto salvation," the second coming to bring full salvation, "unto them that look for him," the waiting believers (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the two appearings. The verse marks the offering: once for sins. The verse displays the sin-bearing: of many. The verse displays the second coming: without sin. The verse displays the goal: unto salvation. The parallel displays Christ's one offering and his return for salvation. The verse displays the offered: once. The verse displays the appearing: second time. The verse displays the salvation: brought. Christ once offered for sins, appearing a second time for salvation, displays the crown of the chapter's argument: as men die once and face judgment, so Christ was offered once to bear the sins of many — and he will appear a second time, no longer to deal with sin but to bring full salvation to those who eagerly await him. The verse displays the second coming for salvation, closing the great chapter on Christ's once-for-all sacrifice and heavenly priesthood.
